speaker: interviewer
text: --four of the interview with Nathan Nothman.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's the end of the war now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Well, at the end of the war, I went to the camp, Ainring. There were other survivors in Ainring. About a mile and a half from Laufen. There was a mental hospital, but there was nobody there. There was only the survivors from concentration camp. They marched- they was sick. And there was a lot of hospital-- or the doctors were there to help them. Then, one day, I saw Sonia's two sisters came to the camp. And they were walking. And they passed our building there where we sleep. So we talked to them. And we found out that they are Jewish survivors. They're looking for their brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, OK, I will take you there. So we took a car. We have a car. We drove it to the camp. And that's all. I did nothing about it. Next time, my son and my wife came in with another sister. And right away, something happened. I would say, what's going on? Without looking on her, I fell in love. And from that time, I tried to teach her how to ride with the bike. And I have a motorcycle at that time. And we were in the camp all that time, most of the time. But the camp was not too far.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they did transfer the camp to Ainring about 12 kilometer, to-- to the-- closer to [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and Berchtesgaden. Berchtesgaden was a beautiful neighborhood. Beautiful, beautiful thing where Hitler lived. Hitler lived a little further.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, I was-- I was in a camp as a-- [INAUDIBLE] is the camp that I lived at. But I lived in Laufen, but I play soccer there. So for this, they give me-- that I lived there-- and I become that packages that-- chocolate, beef, you know, cigarettes, you know? So we can trade and buy something for ourself. So I met Sonia. We fell in love. And I played soccer there. So we got married in 1947, in January, 26. And then we moved to Laufen, but I still payed soccer in Ainring. And from Ainring, I met the whole family. You know that they're coming to me all the time, you know, because I lived outside camp. They sleep by us. It was a very, very good relation with the whole family, with the sisters, with my wife's-- Sonia's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia's sisters and a brother, whole relation. Then I found out that my mother-- that my mother is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took a trip with Sonia all the way to Bergen-Belsen, all the way to the end of the Germany. I found out that my mother was on a ship, and my sister. 1945, Greve Bernadotte, from Sweden. He was not the UN-- he was the League of Nations at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans took the [INAUDIBLE], and they wanted to dynamite ship and sink the ship all the way to the bottom, between the Sweden coast and Bergen-Belsen. So [INAUDIBLE] went in and talked to the German police. You know, do it-- no. You know? And somehow, he persuaded them to let the ship go to Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took my mother to Sweden and my sister. They were very sick. They went to the hospital. They recovered. And they were working in Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they been together at Plazow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All the camp, yeah. My mother with my sister were all together at Plazow. But I separate-- I went someplace else. And they were in Sweden. I found out that my sister is there, [INAUDIBLE]. She come to visit me. And I tell her that I am going to-- first, I want to go to Israel. But my wife's sister and brother said, no, let's be together. We have no parents. We need each other. Let's go to United States. So I register as a plumber. So I got right away an affidavit. And so we came to United States. And my sister was in Sweden. So I want to bring my sister. I brought my sister from Sweden to United States, 1952, when the election was there. My sister came here. Then my mother came from Sweden to United States, but she couldn't cope. She got mentally sick and she perished. I mean, she die right here in Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just go back for a minute. You came over in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you flew over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Flew over. I flew over from Germany, from Bremen-- Bremen Airport. I forgot what was it. We flew into Iceland-- Iceland. And from Iceland was 1,500 miles to New York. Between that flight, our plane was burning fire. The one engine was burning about 50 feet of flame. When I opened that curtain, I saw the flame, I just put the curtain back. I didn't want to look at it. And the plane going back and forth, back and forth. And a lot of people got sick on the plane. And like I would say, we arrived in New York. And there was a lot of fire station there. They spray foam rubber on the all engine, whole plane. And they told us quicker to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I figure, in the plane, that's it. I survived concentration camp, and then I will die in an ocean. That's it. It cannot be. It's impossible. But somehow, we-- we landed safely in New York. So we came 1950 in New York. And who were you with on the plane?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the plane were a lot of people-- my wife, my daughter, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She was six weeks old. That's why we flew. We couldn't go with the ship. Six weeks old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you made that journey with a six-week-old.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. It was very hard for me. When I came to New York-- we have food from the German. But when I come to the United States, it's different thing. What we took-- we took a liter-- a liter of food for the baby, for my daughter. Now the food was all right, because we flew for 24 hours. 24 hours it took us to fly from United States to New York. So the last thing, we couldn't get nothing to eat. She have nothing to eat, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we tell the social worker, we need something to food. My baby crying. I don't know what happen. She went to a store, and she brought us a glass of milk. And when baby drink the milk, she throw everything up in a second. So my wife said, now, this is milk. We cannot do it. So they put us took a taxi. They sent us to Broadway, 100 Broadway, Hotel Hamilton. And right away, I went in and I cooked it. Because we got it-- we got the-- that left-over special thing what we took from Germany. So we cooked it, and we give our daughter to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah. She was number one. And then, we went to Coney Island, to my sister, to my wife's sister. I was supposed to go to New York, from Coney Island, go to New York, go to the doctor. You should give it a different kind of food. The Jewish organization, they-- they put everything together. They tell me, go to this doctor, this address. Go there. The doctor will give you the thing. Go to the drug store, buy it. And that's it. But I was far away. So I memorized where to go. Went from Coney Island to Times Square.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Coney Island to Times Square?

speaker: interviewee
text: Time Square, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you been in the US for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days, four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, for big city-- in your big city, you always memorize. Went to the doctor, got the prescription. Went to the drug store, buy the food. Took the-- took the subway all the way to Coney Island, and come home. And my wife said, if Nathan doesn't bring the food, we don't know what to do. And I came in. So we tried to cook the food. And it's different thing with milk, with thing. And things we always remember. So. And after this, you know, we came to-- you know, I didn't like to-- I didn't like it to be New York, so my sister-in-law, Regina, said to my wife, please, I have nobody. I want to be with somebody. Come with your baby. Come with Sandy-- it's my daughter-- come with you in Detroit. I want you to come to Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my wife said, all right, I'm going to Detroit. I said to my wife, no, I want to stay in New York. But somehow, I said, OK. I [INAUDIBLE] up. We're going to Detroit. I came here. And in two days, I got a job-- ice cream factory. I was the mechanic. I'm supposed to watch that the machine is good, that the oil and everything's working fine, because I was-- I was mechanic-- a plumber. Plumber is-- he can do almost everything, because we have to do with fire, with water, electricity, you name it, breaking the concrete, fixing the concrete, break the dry wall, fix the dry wall. So you know, sometimes, repainting the walls, you know? So that's just like a general contractor. So I was really good. And I worked there for a year, and I didn't want to work. I said, I want to be a plumber. So I went to a company, and I worked $1 an hour. Already, I got $1.87 an hour. And I work overtime every day. I brought [INAUDIBLE] $100 a week check home.

speaker: interviewee
text: This week, I brought $40 a week check. So I said to my boss, I cannot work for this money. I'm working three weeks already, I want a raise. I want you to give me piece work. He said, all right, I'll give you piece work. You do this, this, this, you get paid. So I wind up $80, $90 a week wages, but I work already-- he knew that I knew. The plumbing, the only thing-- the same threads-- is the difference a little bit. But when you're a mechanic, you know, you get it. And he knew that I work, so I make my-- I worked for him for a year. And then the $100, $120, $150-- some were $200. But I said, give me small job for myself too. I need money. So he was good. So he give me-- this is your job. Go make it. Go do it. Make some money. And then I live on [? Dexter ?] there. So some people need plumbing, so I work. And then I went out of him, and I went for license. I got a general license. Then I work already for myself. In 1955, I got my plumbing license. And then-- I was ambitious. I want to work for myself. I didn't want to work for nobody. I didn't want nobody tell me what to do. That's enough for five years. I want to be my own boss.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we work. And then we moved to another place. From there, we moved to [? Aukburg. ?] From [? Aukburg, ?] we got into West Bloomfield. And then I'm here. And you've built this beautiful home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I built myself a house there. And everybody-- and I have grandchildren, I build a-- and my daughter lived next to me. The house what I built for me, I built for my children a house together.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Sandy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandy, my three grandchildren. We raised them together. We were Friday night supper. Every Friday night is supper in our house, all the children, grandchildren, friends sometimes, my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son, Sammy?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Sammy. He should be here 1 o'clock. He promised me. Handsome, tall guy, six-footer. You'll see. And nice. Good. We give the children, education is number one. My-- My daughter, she's a teacher, Sandy. You know, you met Carol. She master degree. She's ambitious. My son went to college. And he said, Daddy, I want to be a plumber. I don't want to sit in a chair and-- and figure thing. And I said listen, trade is good. You want to be a plumber, go ahead. And he said, I'm [INAUDIBLE] be a plumber. This way, you're not supposed to sit. You're not supposed to be worried about the job. You want to go vacation, you can go-- you can go vacation. And he likes the job, because every day, someplace else. Or sometimes, he stay two, three days on a job or week on a job. So you go there. And it's a very, very good trade. Like my doctor said, you plumbers charging more than we. When I come to a person, it's $50, $55 service call, plus this, and this. And the doctor said, I cannot charge $55. I charge $30, $35. But I told him difference. I'm coming to you with my truck, with my dirty clothes, with tools, with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, you've been in the United States for 45 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: 45 years, plus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you think back, what-- you've really had quite a life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've had quite a life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish-- I'd like to make a comment. I wish the United States-- in the whole war, there was no United Nations. There was a League of Nations. That no difference what kind of skin you have, what kind of religion you have, to which shul, or which synagogue, or which church, or anything. We should be together. We should always work together to bring better world for us. Because United States made a big blunder, and England, the same thing. If United States would accept the Jews to send them to Madagascar, a lot of the Jewish people would survive. Not just the Jewish, one million gypsies, or 300,000, 400,000 Jehovah Witness, and communists, non-communist, anybody who is against the German. Anybody who said no was executed. They were raping and killing people like killing fly in July. And United States allowed this to happen. 1944, when that-- when Hungary send the message to United States, there's 700,000 Jews. Take them out, because Germany will come in. They will send them to Auschwitz. Come and do it. Well, we have no place. We don't have no ships. We're supposed to make a meeting. By the time we try to do something, we cannot do it. They didn't want to do it. President Roosevelt didn't want to do it, because the oil. In Middle East was oil, always more than anything. There was a place in-- in the Middle East and Palestine to send a million, 2 million Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it doesn't matter what you eat, so long as you're alive. Hitler didn't want to give the Jews out to the United States and to-- and to England, or anything. So they said, well listen-- said to Hitler-- OK, you keep the Jews with you. And that's it. So like somebody giving the green light to do whatever do. If United States, or England, or the free world would say, now listen, Switzerland, even-- even Sweden-- Sweden make money with German, in the billions. Switzerland in the billions make money. Switzerland, up to 1943, they closed the border to the Jews. Swiss too. Did not allow Jews to go to Sweden and to Switzerland. When the Stalingrad was surrender, and they surrender with-- and they caught Stalingrad. And they knew that the Russian beating the German. And United States in Desert Fox-- and it's in Africa, of course-- surrender, the Sweden and Switzerland, for now that the war is over, because they're losing ground, they try to open the border. Swiss tried to open the border for the Jews from Belgium, from Holland, you know, to transfer the Jews. Why didn't open the border in 1940, '41, '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: Switzerland sent a lot of the Jews back. When they went enter into Switzerland, they said, I'm sorry. We cannot hold you. We send you back. They knew that the Jews would go to Auschwitz, and they would be killed. They didn't care. I read in a paper that the Switzerland, the minister-- the prime minister from Switzerland-- or I forgot what was it-- apologize what they done to the Jews. Apologize. That's why our President say, never again. If there would be any trouble, killing innocent people, we should step in. We should not allow that. Our President made this, can't allow this.

speaker: interviewer
text: If United States President, or a French, or anybody said, now listen, you cannot kill the Jews. Because when we come to Germany, we will execute-- not just the Nazis, but the women too, because the women are guilty. We will execute women, and your children, and everybody. OK, let's put in as a bluff, but it would work. The German women would be afraid. Well, what's the matter? If they come here, they will get my son, and they will execute me. The women play a big role for the Nazis. They pushed them hard, so they go higher and higher.

speaker: interviewee
text: But United States didn't say nothing. England didn't say nothing. England didn't care for the Jews. They didn't care. United States President, he didn't care. There's a dispatch came. A courier came from Poland to England, from England to Warsaw ghetto. They smuggle into Warsaw ghetto, and they told them what happened. It was [INAUDIBLE], . He was talking to us. It was right in Detroit. And they told us the whole thing. And then he went back to England and flew to United States. And he told the United States President, they're killing the Jews by the thousand, by the millions. Listen, you must do something. Tell them that we'll be there. Don't worry. Tell them that we'll be there. They didn't want to help nothing, so it's no good. You know, there was a-- I don't know-- there's a priest or something, he put it nicely. When they come-- when they come for the Jews, the German, nobody complain. Oh, someone could take the Jews. That doesn't matter. But when Hitler was through with the Jews, then he come for the Gentile. So now, the other religious, Muslim, anybody, said, oh, we don't care. So they take the Gentile. Big deal. But when they come to the-- to the [INAUDIBLE], they said, we made a mistake. There's nobody to complain. We should start from the beginning to complain right away. Because if you think that the Jews-- he's still with the Jews, with the Gentile, and with the other religions, that's what's wrong. We should stop and complain right away. So this is the explanation, that nobody cared for us. Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's a song I like to sing. There's an English song-- I'm not Frank Sinatra, but I like to sing. Let them know that the song is an English too, but-- the name is 'Tell Me, Where Can I Go?' Do you have that song there? [SINGING] Tell me, where can I go? There is no place I can see where to go, where to go. Every door is closed from me, and to the left and to the right. At the same in every land, there is nowhere to go. It is me who should know. Won't you please understand? There is nowhere to go. It is me who should know. Won't you please understand? Now I know where to go, where my [INAUDIBLE] proudly stay. Let me go. Let me go to the precious promised land. And no more left, and no more right. If I hadn't pulled the line. I am proud, can't you see? For at least I am free. No more wandering for me. I am proud, can't you see? For at least I am free. No more wandering for me. Thank you, very much, Mr. Nothman.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the song.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just don't blink your eye. Remember, for the next eight minutes, no blinking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or breathe.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] No breathing. Right. Mr. Nothman, I see two beautiful women by your side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Sonia Nothman. And this is my daughter, Carol Patton. And they are here, if they like to say something. You can ask them, maybe. Why not?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Nothham, would you like to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad my daughter joined us, now she can. Which I seldom see her. And I'm very happy, overjoyed. And she's here while we're making the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives in Philadelphia. She came. It's a long way for her. I know she works, and I do appreciate seeing her. And she listened to all those thing, which I seldom speak to them when they grew up. But I guess, now, she will know everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Carol Patton?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. I'm very proud of my parents and all of the things they have accomplished. It was a pretty big accomplishment, at least in my eyes. And I'm very grateful to have this opportunity to have their memories recorded for future generations to understand and learn from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nathan, did you think that this was going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. If somebody would tell me in 1940 that I would be married to my lovely wife, have beautiful children, grandchildren, live in United States, have a house, have a trade, never hungry, and free, I would not believe it. But when you're alive, you can accomplish everything. And I accomplished, with my lovely wife, with the children. So that's why I'm here. You know, it's possible. Everything is possible when a person is alive. And I thank everybody that I'm here, especially my wife, because she's a very, very supportive woman. And she's a very good wife--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, for the compliment.

speaker: interviewee
text: --mother, understandable. And she's creating job, you know, helping Holocaust to come and to speak to the children. And she's very good in it. I know it. Somebody told me already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're helping. We're helping people to understand what happened to us. And I hope, in the future, that we'll forget what kind of skin I have. Black, or white, or green, or yellow, we're human being. We should help each other. And if we help each other, that would be beautiful world to live in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we would not help each other, where we [INAUDIBLE] against religious, against where you come from, who you come, he's black, he's white, he's green, then we have a disaster in this country. I hope that people understand it's very, very good to help each other. That's what we're doing. And I hope that we would live to see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Nothman, would you tell us something about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo, it's me in the picture. The photo was taken in 1946, or '47. '46, probably. And that was in Laufen, when I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: It took me a long time before I put my hair in shape, you know? Because my hair was half inch tall. And I done a good job on it, I think so. And that's me, 1947, before I got married. Mr. Nothman, what is this certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is anybody who belonged to the Jewish Athletic Club, you know, soccer game. We got like a registration that I belonged to that organization, and I played soccer for this special club, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you open it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my picture. And that was in 1947, January, 1947. That we got, in the camp, anybody who play for the soccer must have a registration and must belong to a camp. So now, they register him as a camp. And then, I got my pictures and that things that I play soccer for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph-- before we play-- before we played, some of us to make picture. And this is the whole 11 or 12 with the coach, before we play soccer. So we made a lot of pictures. And they're all my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are forth from the last?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am there, from my left, one, two-- the fourth one there.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture-- we lived by photographer in Laufen when I lived in a small city. This is my sister, to my left, Regina [? Moskowitz ?], and my wife, and myself. And this was 1946, end of '46, in Laufen. Quiet. This is the wedding day, January, 1947, January the 26th. This is the picture with my wife sitting. To my right, is my sister-in-law, Helen, Regina [? Moskowitz ?], my friend, my brother-in-law, my sister-- sister, [? Bella Soloway, ?] and my brother-in-law, and my-- and my niece. She was maybe about three months old. This is picture in my house. My mother came from Sweden to visit me. And they're all my friends. They're my brother-in-laws. They're my sister-in-laws, my wife, my mother, myself, my friend, and my sister and my brother-in-law. That was 1948 in Laufen where I lived in Germany. This is a New Year, what we send it in Germany. We lived by photographer. And we told them that you should make a picture with my wife, put in a circle so we can sent it to our friends. And they would see the picture who sent them. And there was a greeting for New Year, for every year we were in Germany. This is the picture for my daughter, Carol, 1985. To my right is my son-in-law, my granddaughter, my daughter, Scott, my grandson, and [? Marty ?], my granddaughter. There's myself. There's my son-in-law. This is Carol. This is my wife, Sonia. This is my daughter-in-law, Nancy, and my son, Sam.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a very happy occasion.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very, very happy occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Nothman, you have a beautiful, beautiful family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do. So from nothing-- yeah.
